title: Dog Ingested Xylitol Gum: Immediate Emergency Action Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate sugar-free gum containing xylitol, this is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is essential for survival.
content_html:
TL;DR: Xylitol ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that causes rapid blood sugar drops and potential liver failure; seek immediate veterinary care if your pet consumes any sugar-free products.

What is xylitol and why is it dangerous for dogs?

Xylitol is a sugar alcohol commonly used as a sweetener in sugar-free chewing gum, candies, and breath mints. While perfectly safe for human consumption, it is a potent toxin for dogs. When a dog ingests xylitol, it triggers a rapid and massive release of insulin from the pancreas, leading to a dangerous drop in blood sugar levels.

Is xylitol ingestion in dogs a medical emergency?

  • Contact your local emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately, as this is a life-threatening emergency.
  • Do not "wait and see," because ingestion requires immediate veterinary intervention.
  • Watch for symptoms of profound hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) within 30 to 60 minutes, which can lead to seizures, coma, and death.
  • Be aware that in higher doses, xylitol can cause acute, irreversible liver failure.

How does a photo of the product packaging help my veterinarian with triage?

  • Safely take a clear photo of the gum packaging, specifically the ingredient list and the nutritional information.
  • Note that different brands contain varying concentrations of xylitol, which impacts the severity of the case.
  • Provide the photo so your veterinarian can calculate the exact toxic dose based on your dog's weight.
  • Help the medical team determine if the treatment plan requires basic monitoring or intensive life-support measures.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, xylitol stimulates a rapid, dose-dependent insulin release, potentially causing profound hypoglycemia. Dosages exceeding 75-100 mg/kg can induce hypoglycemia, while dosages over 500 mg/kg may lead to severe hepatic insufficiency or failure. Hypoglycemia can manifest within 30 minutes of ingestion, or be delayed up to 12-18 hours. Clinical signs of hypoglycemia include vomiting, weakness, ataxia, depression, hypokalemia, seizures, and coma. Liver injury signs may appear 24-48 hours post-ingestion, with liver enzyme increases detectable within 8-12 hours. Due to the potential for rapid hypoglycemia onset, emesis should only be attempted under veterinary supervision in asymptomatic animals. Activated charcoal is not recommended. Animals ingesting >75-100 mg/kg should be hospitalized for blood glucose monitoring every 1-2 hours for at least 12 hours, while those ingesting >500 mg/kg should have baseline liver values measured, and then evaluated every 24 hours for at least 72 hours. Hypoglycemia should be managed with IV dextrose boluses and/or constant-rate infusions until the dog maintains normal blood glucose without supplementation. Dogs ingesting xylitol at >500 mg/kg should receive dextrose and hepatoprotectants such as N-acetylcysteine, S-adenosylmethionine, and silymarin, even if normoglycemic.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of xylitol poisoning?
faqs.answer: Early signs include vomiting, followed by symptoms of low blood sugar such as lethargy, stumbling, weakness, and collapse.

faqs.question: How much xylitol is toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: As little as 0.1 grams per kilogram of body weight can cause hypoglycemia, while higher amounts can lead to liver failure. One or two pieces of gum can be toxic to a small dog.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian, as it can be dangerous if the dog is already becoming weak or disoriented.
